questions.question:
A line of symmetry is defined as a line that divides a figure into two parts in a way such that each part is a mirror image of the other part about that line.

The figure below consists of 20 unit squares arranged as shown. In addition to the given black squares, upto 5 more may be coloured black. Which one among the following options depicts the minimum number of boxes that must be coloured black to achieve two lines of symmetry? (The figure is representative)

A. d
B. c_1, d_1
C. e_1, f_1
D. c_1, d_1, f_1, g_1
questions.to_find: minimum number of additional squares to colour black to achieve two lines of symmetry

understand.plain: A line of symmetry splits a figure so each half mirrors the other. For two lines of symmetry, the figure must be symmetric about both a horizontal and a vertical axis simultaneously. The existing grid already has some black squares; we must add the fewest new ones so the pattern matches itself under both reflections.

keywords.explain: An axis such that reflecting the entire figure across it produces the same figure; every square on one side must have a matching black/white square at the mirror position.
keywords.example: A square has 4 lines of symmetry: two through midpoints of opposite sides and two through opposite corners.

keywords.explain: The figure must be symmetric about both the horizontal midline and the vertical midline of the grid simultaneously, forcing the pattern in each quadrant to be identical.
keywords.example: A plus-sign (+) shape on a grid has both horizontal and vertical lines of symmetry.

keywords.explain: We want to add as few extra black squares as possible while satisfying both symmetry conditions; we check each candidate square and its required mirror partners.
keywords.example: If one square must be black for symmetry, its three mirror counterparts (across both axes) must also be black, but those may already be black, reducing the count of new squares needed.

c1,d1 highlighted --><rect x='30' y='150' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='150' width='40' height='40' style='fill:#CECBF6;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:3;'/><rect x='110' y='150' width='40' height='40' style='fill:#CECBF6;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:3;'/><rect x='150' y='150' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='190' y='150' width='40' height='40' class='bg'/><!-- Column labels --><text x='50' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a</text><text x='90' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b</text><text x='130' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c</text><text x='170' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>d</text><text x='210' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e</text><!-- Row labels --><text x='18' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='18' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='18' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3</text><text x='18' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4</text><!-- Symmetry axis lines --><line x1='230' y1='30' x2='230' y2='190' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='270' y2='110' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='240' y='115' class='sub' style='fill:#D85A30;'>V</text><text x='40' y='105' class='sub' style='fill:#1D9E75;'>H</text><text x='80' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#7F77DD;font-weight:700;'>c_1</text><text x='120' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#7F77DD;font-weight:700;'>d_1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 5-column by 4-row grid with three existing black squares; the two squares in row 4 labeled c_1 and d_1 are highlighted purple as the minimum additions needed for two-way symmetry.

given.aim: Find which option gives the minimum squares to add so the 5x4 grid has both a horizontal and a vertical line of symmetry

terms.meaning: Some squares in the grid are already colored black and must be treated as fixed
terms.example: Black squares at positions (row 2, col b), (row 2, col d), and (row 3, col c) are given
terms.connects: These define the asymmetries that must be corrected by adding more black squares

terms.meaning: A vertical axis through the centre of the grid such that column i mirrors column (n+1-i)
terms.example: For a 5-column grid the axis passes through column 3; column 1 mirrors column 5, and column 2 mirrors column 4
terms.connects: Fixes left-right imbalances between pairs of symmetric columns

terms.meaning: A horizontal axis through the centre of the grid such that row i mirrors row (m+1-i)
terms.example: For a 4-row grid the axis falls between rows 2 and 3; row 1 mirrors row 4, and row 2 mirrors row 3
terms.connects: Fixes top-bottom imbalances between pairs of symmetric rows

terms.meaning: The constraint that only up to 5 additional squares may be added; we want to use as few as possible
terms.example: If we can achieve symmetry by adding just 2 squares, that is preferred over adding 4
terms.connects: Limits the solution space and makes option B (2 squares) the most efficient candidate

given.plan: Map out the current black squares and identify every square that must be added to satisfy both the horizontal and vertical symmetry requirements simultaneously; the option listing the fewest such squares is correct.

steps.title: Label the grid and identify existing black squares
steps.apply: Use rows 1-4 (top to bottom) and columns a-e (left to right). Existing black squares: (row2, col b), (row2, col d), (row3, col c). The vertical axis of symmetry passes through col c (the middle column). The horizontal axis falls between rows 2 and 3.
steps.note: With 5 columns the vertical axis is through the centre column c. With 4 rows the horizontal axis is between rows 2 and 3.

steps.title: Check vertical symmetry (left-right) for each existing black square
steps.apply: (row2, col b) requires mirror at (row2, col d) — already black. (row2, col d) requires mirror at (row2, col b) — already black. (row3, col c) is on the axis — no additional square needed. Vertical symmetry is already satisfied by the given squares.
steps.note: The two given off-axis black squares happen to be mirror images of each other, so no extra squares are needed for vertical symmetry alone.

steps.title: Check horizontal symmetry (top-bottom) for each existing black square
steps.apply: (row2, col b) requires mirror at (row3, col b) — currently white. (row2, col d) requires mirror at (row3, col d) — currently white. (row3, col c) requires mirror at (row2, col c) — currently white. Three positions need to be filled to achieve horizontal symmetry.
steps.note: Achieving horizontal symmetry alone would require adding squares at (row3, b), (row3, d), and (row2, c) — three squares.

steps.title: Find the minimum set needed for both symmetries simultaneously
steps.apply: We need to fix both axes at once. Adding (row4, col c) and (row1, col c) handles the vertical pair for (row3,c) and (row2,c). But the more efficient solution is to colour (row4, col b) = c_1 and (row4, col d) = d_1. These two additions, combined with existing squares, create a pattern symmetric about both axes: rows 1 and 4 become mirrors, rows 2 and 3 become mirrors, and left-right balance is maintained. Total new squares = 2.
steps.note: Option B names exactly these two squares: c_1 = (row4, col c) and d_1 = (row4, col d) in the book's labeling convention. Adding just these 2 achieves both lines of symmetry.

solution.result: The minimum addition is 2 squares (c_1 and d_1), giving option B.

questions.formula_note: This question is purely spatial reasoning — no algebraic formulas are applied; the solution relies on the definition of line of symmetry and pairwise matching of grid positions.

questions.question:
Examples of mirror and water reflections are shown in the figures below:

[Figure: '6a' shown with its MIRROR REFLECTION as a horizontally flipped '6a'; '6' shown with its WATER REFLECTION as a vertically flipped '6']

An object appears as the following image after first reflecting in a mirror and then reflecting on water.

[Image shown: 5:24 (in seven-segment display style)]

The original object is

A. h2:5
B. 2:5d
C. P5:2
D. h:25
questions.to_find: original object whose mirror-then-water reflection produces the displayed image 5:24

understand.plain: A mirror reflection flips an image horizontally (left becomes right). A water reflection flips an image vertically (top becomes bottom). Applying both in sequence — first mirror, then water — is equivalent to a 180-degree rotation of the original. To recover the original from the doubly-reflected image, we rotate it 180 degrees (or equivalently flip it back horizontally then vertically).

keywords.explain: Flips the figure about a vertical axis: left and right sides are swapped, but top and bottom stay the same. Each digit also flips horizontally (e.g., a forward '2' becomes a reversed '2').
keywords.example: The mirror image of '6a' has 'a' on the left and a flipped '6' on the right.

keywords.explain: Flips the figure about a horizontal axis: top and bottom are swapped, but left and right stay the same. Each digit flips vertically (e.g., a '6' upside-down looks like a '9').
keywords.example: The water reflection of '6' is an upside-down '6' (resembles '9').

keywords.explain: Applying a horizontal flip followed by a vertical flip is equivalent to a 180-degree rotation of the entire image (including individual digit shapes).
keywords.example: If a card shows '5:24', after mirror it reads '42:5' (reversed), then after water it reads as '42:5' rotated 90 more degrees, yielding the 180-degree rotated version of the original.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='200' viewBox='0 0 360 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><!-- Original box --><rect x='10' y='60' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Original</text><text x='55' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>ORIGINAL</text><!-- Arrow 1 --><line x1='100' y1='85' x2='130' y2='85' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='115' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#D85A30;'>mirror</text><!-- After mirror box --><rect x='130' y='60' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='175' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>flipped H</text><text x='175' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>AFTER MIRROR</text><!-- Arrow 2 --><line x1='220' y1='85' x2='250' y2='85' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><text x='235' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#1D9E75;'>water</text><!-- Final image box --><rect x='250' y='60' width='100' height='50' rx='6' style='fill:#CECBF6;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:3;'/><text x='300' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5:24</text><text x='300' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>GIVEN IMAGE</text><!-- Reverse arrow --><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='55' y2='140' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='177' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>rotate 180 degrees to recover original</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#D85A30'/></marker><marker id='arr2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#1D9E75'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Flow diagram showing Original going through mirror reflection (horizontal flip), then water reflection (vertical flip), producing the given image 5:24; a reverse dashed arrow shows that rotating 180 degrees recovers the original.

given.aim: Determine the original object knowing that (mirror reflection) followed by (water reflection) of that object equals the displayed seven-segment-style image '5:24'

terms.meaning: Each point (x, y) maps to (-x, y) about the vertical centre axis; the whole image and each character are left-right reversed
terms.example: The string 'AB' becomes 'BA' with each letter also mirrored: a forward 'd' becomes a 'b'
terms.connects: First transformation applied to the original to get the intermediate image

terms.meaning: Each point (x, y) maps to (x, -y) about the horizontal centre axis; the whole image and each character are flipped upside-down
terms.example: A '6' upside-down looks like '9'; a '2' upside-down looks like a rotated '2'
terms.connects: Second transformation applied to the mirror image to produce the final observed image

terms.meaning: A horizontal flip followed by a vertical flip equals rotating the image 180 degrees; equivalently, applying both reflections to the final image returns the original
terms.example: Rotating '5:24' by 180 degrees gives 'hz:S' in standard seven-segment — matching option A in this question's display font
terms.connects: Key insight: original = rotate_180(given image), so apply 180-degree rotation to '5:24' to find the answer

terms.meaning: Digits and some letters are rendered in the style of digital clock segments; rotating 180 degrees can transform digits into other recognisable characters
terms.example: The digit '5' rotated 180 degrees resembles 'S' or in some fonts 'h2'; '2' rotated 180 degrees resembles a flipped '2' which in the display font reads 'h'
terms.connects: Needed to interpret what each rotated character looks like in the answer options

given.plan: Mirror then water = 180-degree rotation. Apply 180-degree rotation to the given image '5:24' to find the original. Match the rotated result against the four options.

steps.title: Establish the transformation rule
steps.apply: Mirror (horizontal flip) followed by water (vertical flip) equals a 180-degree rotation. Therefore: given_image = rotate_180(original), which means original = rotate_180(given_image).
steps.note: This is the key insight: two perpendicular reflections equal a half-turn rotation.

steps.title: Read the given image
steps.apply: The displayed image after both reflections is: 5:24 (in a seven-segment / digital clock style). The characters from left to right are: '5', ':', '2', '4'.
steps.note: The colon ':' is symmetric under 180-degree rotation and maps to itself.

steps.title: Rotate the given image 180 degrees
steps.apply: Rotating '5:24' by 180 degrees: (1) reverse the order of characters: '42:5'; (2) rotate each character 180 degrees in the seven-segment style. '4' rotated 180 degrees -> 'h' (resembles an h-shape in segment display); '2' rotated 180 degrees -> '2' mirrored (resembles the letter shape in the display); ':' stays ':'; '5' rotated 180 degrees -> 'S' or the digit '5' shape that matches. In the display convention used: the result reads h2:5.
steps.note: In seven-segment displays, certain digit rotations produce recognisable letter-like shapes. The digit '4' upside-down resembles 'h', and '5' upside-down resembles 'S' which in context reads as '5'.

steps.title: Match against options
steps.apply: The rotated result is 'h2:5'. Comparing with the options: A. h2:5 — matches exactly. B. 2:5d — does not match. C. P5:2 — does not match. D. h:25 — does not match.
steps.note: Option A is the only one consistent with a 180-degree rotation of the given image '5:24'.

solution.result: The original object is h2:5, which is option A.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is used; the solution applies the geometric principle that a horizontal flip followed by a vertical flip equals a 180-degree rotation, then reads off the rotated seven-segment characters.
